sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the airways characterised by episodic and reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and excessive mucus production. Acute exacerbations are marked by a rapid worsening of symptoms due to airway inflammation, smooth muscle contraction (bronchoconstriction), and increased mucus secretion, which together can progress to respiratory failure if untreated. During an asthma attack, the airways become inflamed, oedematous, and narrowed, often in response to specific triggers such as allergens, respiratory infections, or irritants. This pathophysiological process results in symptoms including wheezing, cough, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. In chronic or poorly controlled asthma, persistent inflammation may lead to airway remodelling and irreversible airflow limitation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Tachypnoea, dyspnoea, wheeze, cyanosis
- Chest Tightness, accessory muscle use, tripod positioning
- Tachycardia, diaphoresis
- Shortening answers/replies to questions i.e., one-word answers

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- If doubt exists as to whether the patient has asthma or anaphylaxis, treat as anaphylaxis
- Severity should be judged from work of breathing, speech, mental state, respiratory effort, exhaustion, and response to treatment rather than pulse oximetry alone
- A quiet chest, exhaustion, altered mental status, cyanosis, poor respiratory effort, or bradycardia should be treated as life-threatening asthma
- Respiratory symptoms such as dyspnoea and wheeze are non-specific, so differentials such as anaphylaxis, heart failure, COPD, foreign body, or pneumonia should be considered
- Patients with acute asthma may deteriorate rapidly without warning
- If assisted ventilation is required, avoid rapid over-ventilation and allow a prolonged expiratory phase to reduce air trapping
- Relievers are often best delivered by MDI and spacer when the patient can cooperate, with repeated reassessment after each treatment cycle

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient upright or in another position of comfort if tolerated
- Perform frequent vital signs, especially respiratory rate, work of breathing, and oxygen saturation
- Give oxygen only as clinically indicated and titrate to need
- Administer salbutamol by spacer or nebuliser according to local protocol and patient capability
- If the presentation is severe or life-threatening, escalate early and consider adrenaline in line with local protocol and senior consultation
- If ventilation is required, allow adequate exhalation and avoid rapid over-ventilation, which may worsen air trapping, hypotension, and cardiac arrest
- Be alert for rapid deterioration

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ipratropium bromide if indicated within local protocol
- Consider IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1 mg/mL solution, i.e. 0.5 mL) for life-threatening asthma in adults in line with local protocol and senior consultation. Note: an adrenaline autoinjector (EpiPen) delivers only 0.3 mg and is subtherapeutic for life-threatening asthma; draw-up IM injection is the preferred route when available
- Consider magnesium sulphate for severe or life-threatening asthma that is not responding adequately to first-line treatment, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Gain vascular access if advanced therapy is likely to be required
- Consider corticosteroid therapy in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider IV fluids only if hypotension is present or another cause of shock is suspected

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Differential diagnosis to be considered:**
  - Cardiac Failure
  - Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
  - Foreign body
  - Smoke inhalation
